title: Baby Sleep Hours Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Recommended sleep hours for babies and infants by age. Find out how much sleep your baby needs.
intro: The Baby Sleep Hours Calculator tells you exactly how much total sleep your child needs each day — broken down into nighttime sleep and daytime naps — based on their age in months. Sleep needs change dramatically in the first three years of life: a newborn requires 14–17 hours per day, while a toddler at 24 months needs just 11–14 hours. The American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM), endorsed by the CDC and AAP, publishes evidence-based recommendations that form the basis of this tool. Enter your baby's age in months to instantly see the recommended total sleep window, number of naps, and nighttime sleep target — so you can build a schedule that supports healthy brain development, immune function, and growth.
keyTakeaway: **Total Sleep/Day = Nighttime Sleep + (Nap Duration × Number of Naps)** — e.g., a 6-month-old needs ~14–15 hrs: 10–11 hrs overnight + 2–3 naps of ~1.5 hrs each (≈3–4.5 hrs of daytime sleep).
useCases: Parents building a daily nap-and-bedtime schedule for a 4-month-old transitioning out of newborn sleep patterns
Pediatricians counseling families on whether a 9-month-old's reported 11 hours/day of total sleep is within the healthy range
Sleep consultants determining the optimal wake-window length between naps for a 12-month-old to prevent overtiredness
Caregivers in daycare settings verifying that nap policies align with AAP-recommended sleep totals for infants 6–18 months old
Parents tracking a toddler's nap-drop milestone (e.g., 18-month transition from 2 naps to 1 nap) against clinical benchmarks
NICU follow-up teams assessing sleep adequacy for premature infants adjusted to their corrected gestational age
seoKeywords: how much sleep does baby need
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

Sleep recommendations are not arbitrary — they come from systematic reviews of polysomnography studies, parental sleep diaries, and developmental outcome research compiled by the American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) and adopted by the CDC.

The calculator applies the following logic:

```
Given: age_months (input)

1. Look up AASM recommended range → [min_hours, max_hours] per day
2. Nighttime sleep = Total sleep − (avg_nap_duration × n_naps)
3. Wake window = (Waking hours in day) ÷ (n_naps + 1)

Example — 6 months:
  Total recommended  = 14–15 hours/day
  Naps               = 2–3 naps × ~1.5 hours = 3.0–4.5 hours daytime
  Nighttime sleep    = 14.5 − 3.75 (midpoint) ≈ 10.75 hrs ≈ 10–11 hrs
  Wake window        = (24 − 14.5) ÷ (2.5 + 1) ≈ 2.7 hrs between naps
```

The calculator outputs the **midpoint** of the recommended range as the primary target, and flags values outside ±1 hour of the range as "below" or "above" typical needs.

---

## Reference Table

| Age | Total Sleep/Day | Nighttime Sleep | Daytime Naps | Avg Nap Duration | Wake Window |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0–1 month | 14–17 hrs | 8–9 hrs | 4–5 naps | 30–60 min | 45–60 min |
| 2–3 months | 14–16 hrs | 9–10 hrs | 3–4 naps | 45–90 min | 60–90 min |
| 4–5 months | 14–15 hrs | 10–11 hrs | 3 naps | 60–90 min | 1.5–2 hrs |
| 6–8 months | 14–15 hrs | 10–11 hrs | 2–3 naps | 1–2 hrs | 2–3 hrs |
| 9–11 months | 12–15 hrs | 10–12 hrs | 2 naps | 1–1.5 hrs | 2.5–3.5 hrs |
| 12–15 months | 12–14 hrs | 10–12 hrs | 1–2 naps | 1–2 hrs | 3–4 hrs |
| 16–18 months | 11–14 hrs | 10–12 hrs | 1 nap | 1–2 hrs | 4–5 hrs |
| 19–23 months | 11–14 hrs | 10–12 hrs | 1 nap | 1–1.5 hrs | 5–6 hrs |
| 24–36 months | 11–14 hrs | 10–13 hrs | 0–1 nap | 1–1.5 hrs | 5–6 hrs |

*Source: AASM 2016 Pediatric Sleep Duration Consensus; CDC Infant & Toddler Sleep.*

---

## Typical Cases

### Case 1 — 3-Month-Old (Growth Spurt Phase)
A 3-month-old should average **14–16 hours/day**. A typical schedule: 9.5 hrs at night + 3 naps of ~1.5 hrs = 14 hrs total. If the baby is sleeping only 11 hours, that is a **26% deficit** relative to the minimum recommendation. Consistent undersleeping at this age is linked to elevated cortisol and impaired myelination (NIH, 2019).

### Case 2 — 6-Month-Old (Classic "Sleep Regression")
Target: **14–15 hours/day**, 2–3 naps. Many parents report the 6-month regression, where the baby temporarily drops to 11–12 hrs. The calculator flags this as below range and recommends capping wake windows at 2.5–3 hours to encourage biological sleep pressure. Nap consolidation (moving from 3 to 2 naps) typically occurs between 6 and 8 months.

### Case 3 — 18-Month-Old (Nap Transition)
At 18 months, most toddlers transition from 2 naps to 1 nap. Total need: **11–14 hours/day**. A healthy schedule: 11 hrs overnight + 1 nap of 1.5–2 hrs = 12.5–13 hrs. Parents who eliminate the nap entirely at this age may observe behavioral dysregulation — studies show 18-month-olds without naps have **34% higher afternoon cortisol** levels (Kurth et al., University of Colorado, 2012).

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Counting only nighttime sleep as "total sleep."** Many parents report "my baby sleeps 10 hours" when they mean 10 hours at night — but daytime naps are *part* of the daily total. A 6-month-old with 10 hrs of night sleep and 4 hrs of naps is actually meeting the 14-hr recommendation perfectly.

2. **Dropping naps too early to "fix" nighttime wake-ups.** Counterintuitively, overtired babies wake more at night due to elevated cortisol. Eliminating a nap at 9 months (before biological readiness at 12–18 months) typically worsens nighttime sleep within 2–3 days.

3. **Ignoring wake windows and focusing only on clock times.** Sleep pressure (adenosine buildup) is age-dependent. A 4-month-old cannot sustain alertness for more than ~2 hours; forcing a 3-hour wake window causes cortisol spikes that shorten nap duration and fragment nighttime sleep.

4. **Treating the recommendation range as a pass/fail cutoff.** The AASM ranges reflect population-level data. Individual babies may naturally sleep 30–45 minutes outside the range and show no developmental concerns. The key indicators are mood, growth percentiles, and feeding cues — not the number alone.

5. **Using adult sleep logic (later bedtime = sleeps later in the morning).** In infants, a bedtime after 8 PM consistently leads to *earlier* morning wake-ups due to cortisol release at biological dawn (~5–6 AM), regardless of when the baby fell asleep.

---

## Related Calculators

- Explore growth milestones alongside sleep with the [Baby Weight & Height Percentile Calculator](/baby-weight-height-percentile)
- Track feeding frequency relative to wake windows with the [Baby Feeding Schedule Calculator](/baby-feeding-schedule)
- Plan your return-to-work schedule around nap times with the [Work Hours Calculator](/work-hours-calculator)

faq.q: How many hours should a 6-month-old sleep per day?
faq.a: According to the American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) — the guidelines adopted by the CDC — a 6-month-old needs **14–15 hours of total sleep per day**. This typically breaks down into 10–11 hours of nighttime sleep and 3–4.5 hours spread across 2–3 daytime naps of approximately 1.5 hours each. Sleeping fewer than 12 hours or more than 16 hours consistently warrants a conversation with your pediatrician.

faq.q: Do naps count toward the total daily sleep recommendation?
faq.a: Yes — the AASM recommendations always refer to **total sleep in a 24-hour period**, including all naps. For a 9-month-old with a target of 12–15 hours/day, 11 hours of nighttime sleep plus two 1.5-hour naps (3 hours) equals 14 hours total — right in range. Separating nap sleep from night sleep is one of the most common misunderstandings parents have when assessing their child's sleep adequacy.

faq.q: When do babies drop from 3 naps to 2 naps?
faq.a: Most babies transition from 3 naps to 2 naps between **6 and 8 months** of age, though some high-sleep-need infants maintain 3 naps until 9 months. Signs of readiness include consistently short or refused third naps, difficulty falling asleep at bedtime, and wake windows extending naturally beyond 3 hours. Forcing the drop before biological readiness typically causes overtiredness and increased nighttime waking within 48–72 hours.

faq.q: Is it normal for a 12-month-old to still take 2 naps?
faq.a: Completely normal. While the average transition from 2 naps to 1 nap happens between **12 and 18 months**, with the median around 15–18 months, some toddlers maintain 2 naps until 18 months without any developmental concern. The AAP notes wide individual variation. A 12-month-old taking two 45-minute naps for a daily total of 13 hours is meeting recommendations perfectly. Consult your pediatrician only if the child shows signs of sleep deprivation (extreme fussiness, frequent illness, poor growth).

faq.q: What is a 'wake window' and why does it matter?
faq.a: A wake window is the amount of time a baby can comfortably stay awake between sleep periods before adenosine (the sleep-pressure molecule) builds to a level that causes cortisol release and overtiredness. Wake windows are **age-dependent**: newborns can only tolerate 45–60 minutes; a 6-month-old can handle 2–3 hours; an 18-month-old manages 5–6 hours. Exceeding the wake window consistently leads to a 'wired but tired' state that paradoxically shortens naps and increases night wakings.

faq.q: Can a baby sleep too much? What are the warning signs?
faq.a: Yes, though it is less common than insufficient sleep. Sleeping **more than the upper limit of the AASM range by 2+ hours consistently** (e.g., a 6-month-old averaging 17–18 hrs/day) can signal jaundice in newborns, infections, neurological concerns, or feeding difficulties. In the first 2 weeks of life, the AAP recommends waking newborns every **2–3 hours** to feed if they do not wake naturally, as excessive sleep can mask dehydration or weight loss issues. Beyond the newborn period, occasional long-sleep days are normal after illness or growth spurts.

faq.q: How does the sleep recommendation differ for premature babies?
faq.a: For premature infants, sleep needs are calculated using **corrected gestational age (CGA)** rather than chronological age. A baby born at 32 weeks who is now 4 months old has a CGA of approximately 2 months (4 months − 2 months premature = 2 months corrected), so the 14–16 hours/day recommendation for 2–3-month-olds applies. The CDC and AAP both recommend using CGA for all developmental benchmarks, including sleep, until at least 24 months for babies born more than 2 months early.

faq.q: Does sleep duration affect a baby's brain development?
faq.a: Yes — substantially. During sleep, specifically slow-wave (NREM) sleep, the brain consolidates memories, clears metabolic waste via the glymphatic system, and releases **80–90% of daily growth hormone (GH)**. NIH-funded research (Deoni et al., 2020) found that infants averaging below-recommended sleep showed measurably lower white matter myelination on MRI at 12 months. Chronic sleep restriction in the first year is also independently associated with increased risk of obesity by age 3 (Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, 2014).

faq.q: What bedtime is best for infants and toddlers?
faq.a: Research consistently points to a **bedtime between 6:30 PM and 8:00 PM** for infants and toddlers, aligned with the biological rise in melatonin (dim-light melatonin onset, or DLMO), which occurs roughly 2 hours before natural sleep onset. A 6-month-old with a last nap ending at 4:30 PM and a 2.5-hour wake window should target a 7:00–7:30 PM bedtime. Bedtimes later than 8:30 PM in this age group are associated with shorter total sleep duration, more frequent night wakings, and earlier morning rises — the opposite of what most parents intend.

sources.name: CDC — How Much Sleep Do I Need? (Infant & Toddler Recommendations)
NIH MedlinePlus — Infant and Newborn Sleep
American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) — Pediatric Sleep Duration Consensus (2016)

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
